title: 9.3 and 9.4 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lessons 9.3 and 9.4!

assessment_items.question_content:
**Although technology has frequently driven population growth, which of the following innovations actually caused the global population to *decline*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Technological advancements in warfare enabled the mass production of deadly weapons.
Increased use of technology limits face-to-face interactions, leading to more social isolation.
Rising living costs have led many millennials to reconsider having multiple children.
Technological change has driven people from rural areas to cities, reducing rural populations.

assessment_items.question_content:
Even major companies like Apple and Starbucks sometimes struggle to ensure their products are made ethically. 

**What does this reveal about globalization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Global supply chains are so complex that many companies struggle to monitor every stage of production.
Globalization always leads to exploitation, making ethical sourcing unrealistic in practice.
Ethical sourcing has become less important as global markets focus more on speed, efficiency, and low production costs.
The financial and logistical costs of ethical sourcing usually outweigh the long-term benefits for most global companies.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sally wrote an essay on the impacts of globalization and included the following topics: 

- Lack of labor standards and fair wages in developing countries
- Rising income inequality
- Limited transparency in ethical sourcing

**Which of the following would be the most appropriate title for Sally’s essay?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Social Impact of an Unregulated Workplace
The Environmental Impact of Globalization
The Benefits of a Global Economy
Technology’s Influence on Global Trade

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains Moore’s Law and its connection to the Great Acceleration?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Moore’s Law predicts microchip transistors will double every two years, driving rapid innovation for decades.
An object in motion tends to stay in motion, meaning technological progress cannot be slowed or reversed once it begins.
Every system has two opposing sides, similar to how globalization includes both positive and negative effects.
Moore’s Law predicts that technology slows down over time, eventually limiting global acceleration.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is the time period after 1945 referred to as the Great Acceleration?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It marks a rapid surge in human activity and technological innovation with widespread global impact.
It marks the end of World War II, when the global community began rebuilding infrastructure.
Nations began a nuclear arms race in response to the U.S. use of atomic bombs on Japan.
It refers to the “space race” between the United States and Soviet Union.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why do some scholars believe that technological progress may slow down in the near future?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Technology requires large amounts of energy, and energy resources are limited.
Transistors can only shrink so much before they stop functioning efficiently.
A declining interest in engineering and science fields will prevent future innovation.
Government policies no longer support technological development or innovation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the primary purpose of the Green Revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It aimed to transform farming practices in order to increase crop yields.
It focused on conserving natural resources and protecting the environment.
It sought to develop clean energy alternatives to fossil fuels.
It was designed to slow down urbanization by limiting population growth.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What global challenges of the mid-20th century did the Green Revolution attempt to address?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Feeding a rapidly growing global population through increased agricultural production.
Promoting political and economic stability in developing regions during the Cold War.
Lowering the environmental impact of fossil fuel use through cleaner energy.
Preserving wildlife and natural parks for future generations.

assessment_items.question_content:
While the Green Revolution significantly increased crop production and provided food security for millions, it was not without environmental consequences.


**What is one way the Green Revolution *negatively* impacted the environment?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The widespread use of fertilizers and pesticides damaged local plants, animals, and ecosystems.
Modern farming techniques reduced water use and helped conserve scarce water resources.
Agricultural practices encouraged the cultivation of many different crops, which harmed ecosystems.
Increased food production was unevenly distributed, leaving some populations undernourished.

assessment_items.question_content:
>Where hunger goes, communism follows.

**How does this saying reflect how the Green Revolution was used to counter the spread of communism—sometimes called the “Red Revolution”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Food insecurity made countries vulnerable to political instability, so increasing food supplies helped reduce the appeal of communism.
The Green Revolution focused on environmental protection and brought democratic and communist nations together around shared goals.
The Green Revolution promoted peace by avoiding involvement in global political conflicts.
Multinational food corporations were established inside communist countries to promote capitalism.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Variolations and vaccinations were both used to stimulate the immune system to build an immunity against smallpox. How do they differ?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Variolation uses live smallpox material, while a vaccine uses a weaker version.
A vaccine uses a live disease, and variolation uses a killed version of the germ.
Variolations carry a lower risk of infection than vaccines.
Vaccines are more fatal than variolations.

assessment_items.question_content:
To eradicate smallpox in areas that were difficult to mass vaccinate, doctors used a strategy called *ring vaccination*.  


**Which of the following best describes this strategy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Physicians would locate an infected person and vaccinate everyone that they had close contact with.
Doctors would draw rings around areas on a map and vaccinate everyone within those boundaries.
Countries implemented border closures to prevent people from crossing and spreading the disease.
Individuals that were infected were quarantined from the rest of the population.

assessment_items.question_content:
The poster below was part of the world-wide campaign to eradicate smallpox. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What impact did this vaccination campaign have on the smallpox pandemic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Vintage public health poster from the Chicago Department of Health asking “Is your child vaccinated?” with a large red question mark in the center. The poster states “Vaccination prevents smallpox,” emphasizing early 20th-century efforts to encourage childhood vaccination against smallpox.

choices.content: It increased public support for vaccination, which led to the global eradication of smallpox.
It was viewed as government overreach, leading to fewer vaccinations and wider disease spread.
It was effective only in a few wealthy countries with access to vaccines.
It reduced the severity of smallpox but did not eliminate the disease.
